item.title: About
item.dc:creator: jamie9c7560732e
item.content:encoded:
Hi, I'm Jamie Terrell.

I've been fixing IT problems since before virtualization was cool—30+ years and counting. These days I'm an infrastructure engineer at Pediatrix Medical Group, where I keep Nutanix clusters, Citrix environments, and Azure workloads running for healthcare teams who can't afford downtime.

I'm a Nutanix Technology Champion with certifications across Nutanix (NCP-MCI, NCP-MCA), Citrix (CCP-V, CC-VAD-CC), Microsoft Azure, and CompTIA Security+. Most of my career has been in healthcare IT, where "the system is down" means something different than it does in most industries.

Why this blog?

Over the years I've solved a lot of problems that weren't well-documented anywhere: VMware-to-AHV migrations, Protection Domain-to-Policy conversions, Citrix upgrades that didn't go as planned. The kind of stuff that's hard to find in official docs because it only shows up when you're knee-deep in a change window at 2 AM.

I write what I wish I could've Googled.

Based in Georgia. Opinions are my own.

Get in touch: jamie@nutanixguy.com

item.title: Nutanix 7.5 Just Dropped – Here's What Actually Matters
item.dc:creator: jamie9c7560732e
item.content:encoded:
So Nutanix pushed out AOS 7.5, AHV 11, and Prism Central 7.5 back in December, and honestly? There's some good stuff in here. Like, actually useful stuff—not just marketing fluff.

Let me break down what I think is worth paying attention to.

VM Startup Policies

This one's been on my wish list forever. You know how it goes—cluster reboots, HA event kicks in, and suddenly your app server is trying to connect to a database that hasn't finished starting yet. Then you're scrambling to manually power things on in the right order while someone's asking why the portal is down. Now you can just tell Nutanix "bring up the DB first, then the app tier, then the web servers." Done. Should've had this years ago, but hey, I'll take it.

Elastic VM Storage

Okay this is kinda cool. You can now spin up a VM on one cluster but use storage from a different cluster in your PC domain. Got a cluster that's heavy on compute but light on storage sitting next to one with tons of free space? Now they can share. Not sure how often I'll use it, but it's nice to have options when capacity gets weird.

Cross-Cluster Live Migration Got Better

Used to be if your storage containers had different names between clusters, CCLM would just say nope. That's fixed now. You can pick your target container during migration. Small thing, big quality-of-life improvement.

CVM Secure Access

You can disable SSH to your CVMs and AHV hosts now via the v4 API. Security team will love it. Word of warning though—if you flip this on, cross-cluster live migration and DR live migration break. They need SSH. So maybe don't go full lockdown until you've thought that through.

Guest Customization Actually Works Now

They finally made guest customization profiles reusable. Set up your Windows customization once, use it across clones and templates without re-entering everything. It's the little things.

NGT Improvements

Nutanix Guest Tools now preserves its config when you clone a VM or deploy from a template. No more "oh right, I need to reinstall NGT on all 50 of these." Also bumped support to 2048 VMs per cluster and added some extra metrics like uptime and FQDN.

Prism Central Backup to S3

PC backup and restore now works with any S3-compatible object store, not just Nutanix Objects. MinIO, Wasabi, whatever you've got. More flexibility for DR planning.

Multisite DR Replication

Protection policies now support one source to two or three destination sites. If you're doing multi-geo DR, this simplifies things a lot. No more duct-taping multiple policies together.

The Stuff I Skipped

There's a ton more—UI tweaks, new v4 APIs, IPv6 support, a new Nutanix Infrastructure Manager thing for large-scale deployments. I'm not going to list all 34 Prism Central features because we'd be here all day. Check the https://portal.nutanix.com if you want the full rundown.

Bottom Line

This is a solid release. VM Startup Policies alone will save me headaches. The CCLM and DR improvements are welcome. Nothing earth-shattering, but a lot of practical stuff that makes day-to-day management easier.

If you're still on 7.3 or earlier, might be worth planning your upgrade path.

item.title: Hey, Stop Sleeping on Prism Central's "View By" Dropdown
item.dc:creator: jamie9c7560732e
item.content:encoded:
You know the drill: log into Prism Central, hit the VMs list, maybe search or sort by name/cluster, and move on. Works fine… until you realize you're missing a quick way to spot real issues.

That little "View by" dropdown up top? It's low-key powerful. One click and it reorganizes your whole VM list around whatever attribute you care about—way better than endless filtering. It answers stuff like:

  • Which VMs have no backup policy?
  • Where'd all my snapshot storage disappear to?
  • Why are half my VMs missing categories for Flow/automation?

(Quick note: This is based on PC 7.3.0.6-ish; might look a tad different in newer builds.)

What "View By" Actually Does

It's in the top-right of the VMs list (Compute & Storage > VMs > List). Default is General view.

Normal sorting just shuffles rows. "View by" rebuilds the hierarchy around one key attribute—think instant grouped dashboard. Custom views let you pick up to 10 columns (Name included). There are tons of options (someone counted ~93 attributes), like Power State, Cluster, vCPUs, Snapshot Usage, CPU Ready Time, Protection Status, and a general "Categories" column that dumps all your key:value tags.

The Built-in Views Worth Using

Storage Configuration

Pick this and boom—VMs grouped by RF, encryption, compression, storage policies.

Spot unencrypted VMs for compliance in seconds, check RF2 vs RF3 on critical stuff, see who's skipping compression. Saves hours during audits.

Data Protection

My favorite for DR/backup sanity checks. Groups by policy, shows protected/unprotected clearly, highlights unprotected VMs.

New DB server slipped in unprotected? Old app missing DR? It's right there screaming at you. No more manual spot-checks.

Quick Custom Wins (No Fancy Hierarchy Needed)

Snapshot Hogs

Add "Snapshot Count" or "Snapshot Usage" columns (via picker or custom view), sort descending. Boom—top offenders show up first.

Found test VMs with 30+ forgotten snaps eating 500GB. Run this quarterly; you'll reclaim space fast. If you need deeper dives, hit Operations > Reports.

CPU Ready Time (Proactive Mode)

Add the "CPU Ready Time" column, sort high-to-low.

Catch contention before tickets roll in—great for oversubscribed hosts or noisy neighbors. Weekly glance beats reactive firefighting. Drill to VM Metrics for more.

Categories / Tagging Gaps

Add the Categories column—it shows all assigned tags per VM.

For specific ones (missing AppType? Environment?), use Filters: pick the category key, filter "Does not exist" or blank.

You'll see:

  • Outage quick-spins never tagged
  • Prod promotions without updates
  • Shadow IT sneaking in

Why care? Flow security, automation, chargeback, lifecycle—all break on untagged VMs. Add the column once, filter as needed during monthly reviews.

Quick Tips to Actually Use It

  • Save custom views—they stick in the dropdown.
  • Layer with search/filters (e.g., View by Data Protection + powered-on filter).
  • Routine checks:
  • Weekly: CPU ready + snapshots
  • Monthly: Protection + categories
  • Quarterly: Full storage audit
  • View by vs Group by: View by gives full hierarchy; Group by just collapsible sections in table view.

Bottom line: Stop treating VMs like a flat spreadsheet. One dropdown click and you get answers that used to take reports or clicking through everything.

Try one this week—set up Categories column + filter for blanks, or snapshot sort. Bet you'll find at least one "oh crap" moment.

What weird stuff have you caught with View by? Drop it in the comments.

item.title: The Hidden Power of Prism Central's "View By" Feature
item.dc:creator: jamie9c7560732e
item.content:encoded:
If you're like most Nutanix administrators, your daily routine in Prism Central probably looks something like this: log in, click on the VMs page, maybe use the search bar to find a specific virtual machine, sort by name or cluster, and get on with your day. You're managing your infrastructure efficiently, or so you think.

But what if I told you there's a feature you're probably overlooking that could save you hours of troubleshooting time, instantly reveal compliance gaps, and help you spot performance issues before they become problems? It's been there all along, hiding in plain sight at the top of your VM list.

I'm talking about the "View by" dropdown.

This unassuming feature isn't just another way to sort your VMs—it's a complete reorganization of your infrastructure view that can instantly answer critical questions like: Which VMs aren't protected by a backup policy? What's consuming all my snapshot storage? Which VMs are missing proper categorization for my automation workflows?

Let's explore how this overlooked feature can transform the way you manage your Nutanix environment. (Note: This is based on Prism Central version 7.3.0.6 and similar 7.x releases; features may vary slightly in other versions.)

What Exactly is "View By"?

Before we dive into specific use cases, let's clarify what "View by" actually does. You'll find it in the upper right section of the VM list in Prism Central (under Compute & Storage > VMs > List tab), right near the search bar and filter options. The default view is the General view.

Unlike simple column sorting (which just reorders rows based on a single column's values), "View by" completely restructures how your VMs are displayed. It groups and organizes your entire VM inventory around a specific attribute, giving you an instant visual hierarchy that would take dozens of clicks and filters to recreate manually.

Think of it as switching from a flat spreadsheet view to a dynamic, attribute-focused dashboard—without leaving the VM list page.

For custom views, you can select up to 10 columns to display (including the Name column). While the exact full list of 93 available attributes isn't publicly documented in detail, common selectable options include basics like Name, Power State, IP Address, Cluster, Host, vCPUs, Memory Capacity, Disk Capacity, and advanced ones such as CPU Usage, Memory Usage, IOPS, Latency, Snapshot Count, Snapshot Usage, CPU Ready Time, Protection Status, various storage attributes, performance metrics, and a general "Categories" column that shows all assigned category key:value pairs for each VM.

Common Built-in Views You Should Be Using

Storage Configuration View

One of the most immediately useful built-in views is the Storage Configuration view. Select this from the "View by" dropdown, and suddenly your VMs are organized by their storage-level settings—replication factor, encryption status, compression settings, and associated storage policies.

Why does this matter? This view instantly reveals your storage posture across the entire environment:

  • Compliance verification: Quickly identify which VMs are encrypted vs. unencrypted—critical for regulatory compliance
  • Data protection validation: See at a glance which VMs are running with RF2 vs. RF3, ensuring critical workloads have appropriate redundancy
  • Performance optimization: Identify VMs with compression enabled or disabled, helping you optimize storage efficiency vs. performance tradeoffs
  • Policy adherence: Verify that VMs are following the storage policies they're supposed to, catching configuration drift

I've seen admins spend significant time running reports or checking individual VM settings to answer "which VMs don't have encryption enabled?"—a question that takes 5 seconds with the Storage Configuration view. During compliance audits, this view becomes invaluable for proving that sensitive workloads meet data protection requirements.

Data Protection View

Here's where "View by" becomes a compliance officer's best friend. Switch to the Data Protection view, and Prism Central reorganizes your VMs to show protection status, assigned protection policies, categories, and more—giving you a comprehensive view of your backup and DR coverage.

This view creates clear groupings showing:

  • VMs organized by protection policy
  • Protection status (protected vs. unprotected)
  • Associated categories that might drive protection rules
  • Unprotected VMs (often the most eye-opening group)

For organizations with compliance requirements, audit timelines, or DR SLAs, this is invaluable. Instead of running reports or manually checking each VM's protection configuration, you get an instant visual audit of your entire data protection posture.

I can't count how many times I've used this view during change management reviews and discovered VMs that should have been protected months ago. That newly provisioned database server? The critical application that got moved to production last quarter? They're all sitting right there in the "Unprotected" section, waiting to be caught before disaster strikes.

The ability to see protection policies at a glance also helps you identify inconsistencies—VMs that should be on the same policy but aren't, or workloads accidentally assigned to the wrong backup schedule.

Custom "View By" Power Moves

The real magic happens when you start creating custom views tailored to your specific operational needs. Here are three game-changing custom views every Nutanix admin should have in their toolkit.

Snapshot Usage: Finding Your Storage Hogs

Snapshots are wonderful for development, testing, and quick recovery—until they're not. Orphaned snapshots from forgotten test VMs can consume massive amounts of storage, and they're notoriously hard to track down when you're just looking at a standard VM list.

To analyze snapshot usage effectively:

  1. Go to the VM list and use the column picker (or create a custom View by) to add snapshot-related columns (like "Snapshot Count" or "Snapshot Usage").
  2. Sort the list by these columns in descending order.

The worst offenders will rise to the top. (Note: While not always a direct hierarchical "View by" reorganization for these metrics, this column-based approach works well in the General, Performance, or a custom view.)

This has helped me identify test VMs with 30+ snapshots that developers forgot about, pre-production systems with weeks-old snapshots that were never cleaned up, and that one VM that somehow accumulated 500GB of snapshot data while everyone wondered where the storage went.

Pro tip: Run this check quarterly as part of your housekeeping routine. You'll be amazed at how much storage you can reclaim simply by identifying and removing stale snapshots. For more detailed analysis, consider creating a custom report in Operations > Reports.

CPU Ready Time: Proactive Performance Monitoring

CPU ready time is one of those metrics that most admins know they should monitor, but rarely do until users start complaining about performance. It measures how long a VM is ready to run but waiting for CPU resources—a key indicator of CPU contention and oversubscription issues.

To monitor CPU ready time proactively:

  1. In the VM list, add the "CPU Ready Time" column via the column picker (available in Performance-related views or custom views).
  2. Sort by highest values.

Now you can instantly spot VMs that are experiencing CPU contention, often before users notice degraded performance. This is especially useful in environments where you're pushing CPU oversubscription ratios or running highly variable workloads.

When I see VMs consistently showing elevated CPU ready times, it's time to investigate:

  • Is the host genuinely oversubscribed?
  • Is there a noisy neighbor VM hogging resources?
  • Does this VM actually need more vCPUs, or is the application poorly optimized?

Checking this weekly can help you stay ahead of performance problems instead of firefighting them. For deeper insights, drill into the VM's Metrics tab.

Categories: The Compliance and Automation Secret Weapon

This is perhaps the most powerful customization, yet it's one of the least utilized. If you're using Nutanix Flow for microsegmentation, implementing chargeback policies, or building any kind of automation, VM categorization is critical. But in large environments, VMs without proper categories slip through the cracks constantly.

To find VMs missing categories or check specific ones:

  1. In the VM list, create a custom View by and add the general Categories column (this shows all assigned category key:value pairs as a single column for each VM).
  2. To look for specific categories (e.g., missing "AppType" or "Environment"), use the Filters pane: select the relevant category key and filter for "Does not exist," blanks, or specific values.
  3. Sort or scan the list to identify uncategorized VMs or those missing required tags.

(Note: You can't directly "View by" a single specific category for hierarchical grouping; the Categories column displays overall assigned categories, and filters handle targeted checks for particular ones.)

What you'll typically find is enlightening and sometimes alarming:

  • VMs spun up quickly during an outage that never got properly tagged
  • Test systems that graduated to production without updating metadata
  • Migrated workloads that lost their categorization in the move
  • Shadow IT VMs that were provisioned outside normal workflows

Why this matters beyond just organization:

  • Security policies: Flow microsegmentation rules rely on categories. Uncategorized VMs might not be properly secured.
  • Automation: If your DR failover, backup policies, or orchestration workflows use category-based selection, uncategorized VMs are invisible to these systems.
  • Reporting and chargeback: Financial and compliance reports based on categories will miss uncategorized VMs entirely.
  • Lifecycle management: Automated retention, scaling, or decommissioning policies can't act on VMs they can't identify.

I've seen security audits flag dozens of VMs that weren't being properly protected by Flow policies simply because they lacked the appropriate categories. A quick check with the Categories column + filters would have caught them months earlier.

Multi-category checking: Add the Categories column in a custom view, then use filters to cycle through different category keys during regular operational reviews. Make it part of your monthly or quarterly housekeeping routine—"Category compliance checks" should be on every Nutanix admin's calendar.

Pro Tips & Workflow Ideas

Now that you understand the power of "View by," here are some tips to make it part of your regular workflow:

Save your favorite custom views: Once you've set up a custom view that's useful, Prism Central remembers it. You don't have to recreate complex custom views every time—they'll be available in your dropdown for quick access.

Combine with search and filters: "View by" works beautifully with Prism Central's search and filter capabilities. You can organize by Protection Domain, then filter to show only powered-on VMs, or use the Categories column while filtering for a specific application name. The combinations are powerful.

Build it into operational reviews: Don't just use "View by" for troubleshooting. Make it part of your regular hygiene:

  • Weekly: Check CPU ready time and snapshot usage
  • Monthly: Review data protection coverage and category compliance
  • Quarterly: Full audit of storage configuration and uncategorized VMs

When to use View By vs. Group By: I mentioned at the start that we're focusing on "View by" rather than "Group by." While there's some overlap, here's the quick distinction: "View by" reorganizes your entire view around one primary attribute with a hierarchical structure, while "Group by" adds collapsible groupings but maintains more of the table structure. For the quick operational insights we've discussed here, "View by" tends to be more visually immediate. That said, "Group by" has its own use cases that deserve exploration in a future post.

Don't Just Manage—Visualize

The "View by" feature in Prism Central represents a fundamental shift in how you can interact with your infrastructure. Instead of managing VMs as a long, flat list that you filter and sort manually, you can instantly reorganize your entire environment around the questions you need to answer.

Which VMs aren't protected? View by Data Protection.

Where's all my snapshot storage going? Add Snapshot Usage column and sort.

Are my security categories properly applied? Add Categories column and filter for specifics.

These aren't separate reports you need to generate or complex queries you need to build. They're instant reorganizations of the data you're already looking at—just one dropdown click away.

Your challenge for this week: Pick one custom view from this article and set it up in your Prism Central environment. Check it during your next operational review. I'm willing to bet you'll discover at least one thing that needs attention—a VM that should be protected, a snapshot that should be cleaned up, or a workload that's missing critical categorization.

The "View by" feature has been there all along, quietly waiting to make your job easier. It's time to stop overlooking it and start leveraging it.

What will you discover in your environment?

Have you been using "View by" in creative ways? Found other use cases worth sharing? I'd love to hear about your experiences in the comments below.
```

item.title: Prism Central Tip: Use the “View By” Dropdown for Faster VM Audits
item.dc:creator: jamie9c7560732e
item.content:encoded:
Most admins stick to searching or sorting in the VM list, but the View by dropdown in Nutanix Prism Central is a much faster way to spot issues.

You’ll find it here:

Compute & Storage → VMs → List View → “View by” (top-right dropdown)

Unlike sorting, View by reorganizes the entire VM list into grouped sections, turning a simple table into a quick operational dashboard.

What It’s Good For

This feature helps you answer common operational questions in seconds:

  • Which VMs are not protected by backup or DR policies
  • Where snapshot storage is being consumed
  • Which VMs are missing required categories/tags
  • Whether compliance settings (encryption, RF, etc.) are consistent

Built-In Views Worth Using

Data Protection (Most Useful)

Groups VMs by protection policy and clearly highlights unprotected systems.

Great for:

  • Finding newly deployed VMs missing DR
  • Spotting legacy workloads outside policy

Storage Configuration

Groups VMs by:

  • Replication Factor (RF)
  • Encryption
  • Compression
  • Storage policy

Useful for:

  • Compliance checks
  • Identifying inconsistent storage settings

Quick Custom Checks

These aren’t built-in views, but they’re fast to create.

Snapshot Usage

How to check:

  1. Add Snapshot Count or Snapshot Usage column
  2. Sort descending

Why it matters:
Quickly identifies VMs consuming excessive snapshot storage.

CPU Ready Time

How to check:

  1. Add CPU Ready Time column
  2. Sort high → low

Why it matters:
Helps detect CPU contention and oversubscription before users report performance issues.

Missing Categories / Tags

How to check:

  • Add the Categories column
  • Or filter by missing category keys

Why it matters:
Untagged VMs can break:

  • Flow policies
  • Automation workflows
  • Chargeback tracking

Suggested Routine Checks

Weekly

  • CPU Ready Time
  • Snapshot usage

Monthly

  • Protection status
  • Category compliance

Quarterly

  • Storage configuration audit

Key Takeaway

The View by dropdown turns the VM list from a flat table into a powerful audit dashboard. Using it regularly helps you catch protection gaps, resource issues, and compliance drift — all without running reports.
